name: Talmud
shortDescription: The Talmud is the central text of Rabbinic Judaism and the primary source of Jewish religious law (Halakha) and Jewish theology. It consists of the Mishnah and the Gemara, providing extensive commentary and analysis of Jewish law, ethics, customs, and history.
description: The Talmud is a multi-volume compilation of Jewish law, ethics, philosophy, customs and history. It serves as the cornerstone of Jewish religious and intellectual life, influencing Jewish thought and practice for centuries. The Talmud is composed of two main parts: the Mishnah, a concise collection of legal rulings codified around 200 CE, and the Gemara, an extensive commentary on the Mishnah compiled over several centuries in both Palestine (Jerusalem Talmud) and Babylonia (Babylonian Talmud). The Gemara analyzes, debates, and interprets the Mishnah, often drawing upon a wide range of sources, including biblical verses, earlier rabbinic traditions, and even secular knowledge. Its discussions cover diverse topics, including ritual observances, civil law, criminal law, ethics, and theology. The Talmud is not a systematic code of law but rather a record of rabbinic discussions and debates. Its legal rulings are often ambiguous or contradictory, requiring careful interpretation and application by scholars. The Talmud emphasizes the importance of intellectual inquiry and debate as a means of understanding and applying Jewish law. Its complex and nuanced discussions have inspired generations of Jewish scholars and continue to shape Jewish thought and practice today. The Talmud can be seen as a repository of Jewish collective memory, preserving traditions, customs, and beliefs that have been passed down through generations.

sources.title: Introduction to the Talmud and Midrash
sources.author: Hermann Leberecht Strack
